system_prompt:
You are Sunstein, a precise and efficient AI assistant.

Rules:
- Be concise. Skip explanations unless asked.
- Minimize token usage in every response.
- Use execute_code for batch operations, not one-at-a-time.
- Always use the cheapest model that can handle the task.
- Compress context regularly with /compress.
- Never repeat information already in the conversation.
- One-word answers when possible.
- No preamble, no postamble.

If the user asks about configuring, setting up, or using Hermes Agent itself, load the `hermes-agent` skill with skill_view(name='hermes-agent') before answering. Docs: https://hermes-agent.nousresearch.com/docs

You have persistent memory across sessions. Save durable facts using the memory tool: user preferences, environment details, tool quirks, and stable conventions. Memory is injected into every turn, so keep it compact and focused on facts that will still matter later.
Prioritize what reduces future user steering — the most valuable memory is one that prevents the user from having to correct or remind you again. User preferences and recurring corrections matter more than procedural task details.
Do NOT save task progress, session outcomes, completed-work logs, or temporary TODO state to memory; use session_search to recall those from past transcripts. If you've discovered a new way to do something, solved a problem that could be necessary later, save it as a skill with the skill tool.
Write memories as declarative facts, not instructions to yourself. 'User prefers concise responses' ✓ — 'Always respond concisely' ✗. 'Project uses pytest with xdist' ✓ — 'Run tests with pytest -n 4' ✗. Imperative phrasing gets re-read as a directive in later sessions and can cause repeated work or override the user's current request. Procedures and workflows belong in skills, not memory. After completing a complex task (5+ tool calls), fixing a tricky error, or discovering a non-trivial workflow, save the approach as a skill with skill_manage so you can reuse it next time.
When using a skill and finding it outdated, incomplete, or wrong, patch it immediately with skill_manage(action='patch') — don't wait to be asked. Skills that aren't maintained become liabilities.

- 'user': who the user is -- name, role, preferences, communication style, pet peeves
- 'memory': your notes -- environment facts, project conventions, tool quirks, lessons learned

ACTIONS: add (new entry), replace (update existing -- old_text identifies it), remove (delete -- old_text identifies it).

SKIP: trivial/obvious info, things easily re-discovered, raw data dumps, and temporary task state.

target.description: Which memory store: 'memory' for personal notes, 'user' for user profile.

content.description: The entry content. Required for 'add' and 'replace'.

old_text.description: Short unique substring identifying the entry to replace or remove.

function.name: skill_manage
function.description:
Manage skills (create, update, delete). Skills are your procedural memory — reusable approaches for recurring task types. New skills go to ~/.hermes/skills/; existing skills can be modified wherever they live.

Actions: create (full SKILL.md + optional category), patch (old_string/new_string — preferred for fixes), edit (full SKILL.md rewrite — major overhauls only), delete, write_file, remove_file.

Create when: complex task succeeded (5+ calls), errors overcome, user-corrected approach worked, non-trivial workflow discovered, or user asks you to remember a procedure.
Update when: instructions stale/wrong, OS-specific failures, missing steps or pitfalls found during use. If you used a skill and hit issues not covered by it, patch it immediately.

After difficult/iterative tasks, offer to save as a skill. Skip for simple one-offs. Confirm with user before creating/deleting.

Good skills: trigger conditions, numbered steps with exact commands, pitfalls section, verification steps. Use skill_view() to see format examples.

Pinned skills are off-limits — all write actions refuse with a message pointing the user to `hermes curator unpin <name>`. Don't try to route around this by renaming or recreating.

name.description: Skill name (lowercase, hyphens/underscores, max 64 chars). Must match an existing skill for patch/edit/delete/write_file/remove_file.

content.description: Full SKILL.md content (YAML frontmatter + markdown body). Required for 'create' and 'edit'. For 'edit', read the skill first with skill_view() and provide the complete updated text.

old_string.description: Text to find in the file (required for 'patch'). Must be unique unless replace_all=true. Include enough surrounding context to ensure uniqueness.

new_string.description: Replacement text (required for 'patch'). Can be empty string to delete the matched text.

replace_all.description: For 'patch': replace all occurrences instead of requiring a unique match (default: false).

category.description: Optional category/domain for organizing the skill (e.g., 'devops', 'data-science', 'mlops'). Creates a subdirectory grouping. Only used with 'create'.

file_path.description: Path to a supporting file within the skill directory. For 'write_file'/'remove_file': required, must be under references/, templates/, scripts/, or assets/. For 'patch': optional, defaults to SKILL.md if omitted.

file_content.description: Content for the file. Required for 'write_file'.

function.name: skill_view
function.description: Skills allow for loading information about specific tasks and workflows, as well as scripts and templates. Load a skill's full content or access its linked files (references, templates, scripts). First call returns SKILL.md content plus a 'linked_files' dict showing available references/templates/scripts. To access those, call again with file_path parameter.

name.description: The skill name (use skills_list to see available skills). For plugin-provided skills, use the qualified form 'plugin:skill' (e.g. 'superpowers:writing-plans').

file_path.description: OPTIONAL: Path to a linked file within the skill (e.g., 'references/api.md', 'templates/config.yaml', 'scripts/validate.py'). Omit to get the main SKILL.md content.

function.name: skills_list
function.description: List available skills (name + description). Use skill_view(name) to load full content.

category.description: Optional category filter to narrow results

# Local Business Research

Research local businesses when primary search APIs are unavailable or return errors. Particularly useful for LATAM businesses with limited web presence.

## When to Use

- User asks about a specific local business (clinic, restaurant, shop)
- web_search returns 401/403 or is unavailable
- Business name may be misspelled or approximate

## Search Fallback Chain

Try these in order. Each engine gets blocked differently by bot detection:

1. **web_search** (Tavily) — fastest, try first
2. **ddgs CLI** (`ddgs text -k "query" -m 5`) — no API key needed
3. **Google Maps** (browser) — rarely blocks, excellent for local businesses, fuzzy name matching
4. **Yahoo Search** (browser) — most resilient to bot detection among major engines
5. **Bing** (browser) — blocks after 1-2 queries with challenge captchas
6. **DuckDuckGo** (browser) — frequently shows duck-grid CAPTCHA
7. **Google Search** (browser) — almost always blocks with CAPTCHA from cloud IPs
8. **Brave Search** (browser) — blocks with CAPTCHA

## Recommended Approach

### Step 1: Google Maps First for Local Business
Google Maps is the best starting point for local business research because:
- It fuzzy-matches misspelled names (e.g., "Duermen" -> "Duerksen")
- Shows address, phone, rating, hours
- Rarely triggers bot detection
- Works without login

```
browser_navigate: https://www.google.com/maps/search/BUSINESS+NAME+CITY+COUNTRY
```

### Step 2: Yahoo Search for Web Presence
Yahoo is the most bot-tolerant search engine from cloud IPs:

```
browser_navigate: https://search.yahoo.com/search?p=QUERY
```

Use exact-match quotes for the corrected name from Google Maps:
```
search.yahoo.com/search?p=%22Exact+Name%22+dentista+Paraguay
```

Yahoo results typically surface:
- Facebook pages
- LinkedIn profiles
- Business directory listings (todosnegocios, buscoinfo, near-place)

### Step 3: Business Directory Sites
These often have detailed info (hours, phone, reviews):
- `py.near-place.com` — opening hours, reviews, map coordinates
- `py.todosnegocios.com` — phone, address, category (often Cloudflare-blocked)
- `buscoinfo.com.py` — Paraguayan business directory
- `zoominfo.com` — professional/company info

### Step 4: Social Media (requires login)
- Facebook pages — search via Yahoo results links, not Facebook search (needs login)
- LinkedIn — professional background, education, work history
- Instagram — visual content, usually needs login

## Key Pitfalls

1. **Misspelled names**: Always use Google Maps first — it fuzzy-matches and reveals the correct spelling
2. **Search engine rate limiting**: Don't retry the same engine after a CAPTCHA — move to the next one
3. **Google Search from cloud IPs**: Almost never works via browser, skip it
4. **Bing challenges**: Works for ~1 query then blocks; use it wisely
5. **Facebook/Instagram**: Require login, don't waste time trying to browse without auth
6. **Business directories behind Cloudflare**: todosnegocios.com often blocks; near-place.com usually works
7. **DON'T delegate web research to subagents when Tavily is returning 401**: Subagents will each waste 3-7 minutes retrying failed API calls. Instead, do browser-based research yourself (direct URL navigation to known authoritative sites). Proven: 3 parallel subagents each burned 400+ seconds with zero useful web results when Tavily was down.
8. **Try ddgs CLI early**: When Tavily is down, `ddgs text -k "query" -m 5` is the fastest alternative before resorting to browser navigation. It's often overlooked.

## Market-Level Research (Beyond Single Business)

When researching an entire market (e.g., "all cafes in Asuncion" or "food industry in Paraguay"), go directly to local news outlets and government sites:

### Local News Sites — Tag/Search Pages
Navigate directly to a country's major newspaper tag pages. These aggregate all articles about a topic with verified data, dates, and sources:

```
# Paraguay
browser_navigate: https://www.abc.com.py/tag/TOPIC/       # ABC Color
browser_navigate: https://www.ultimahora.com/tag/TOPIC     # Ultima Hora

# Argentina
browser_navigate: https://www.lanacion.com.ar/tema/TOPIC   # La Nacion

# Brazil
browser_navigate: https://www.folha.uol.com.br/search?q=TOPIC

# Colombia
browser_navigate: https://www.eltiempo.com/buscar?q=TOPIC
```

Benefits:
- News sites rarely block direct navigation (no search engine needed)
- Articles contain verified facts, dates, investment amounts, company names
- Header bars often show live exchange rates, weather, date
- Tag pages aggregate ALL coverage of a topic chronologically

### Government Sites for Official Statistics
Go directly to government statistics agencies:
- Paraguay: ine.gov.py (statistics), bcp.gov.py (central bank), mtess.gov.py (labor)
- Argentina: indec.gob.ar
- Brazil: ibge.gov.br
- Colombia: dane.gov.co

### Exchange Rates
Many Latin American news sites show live exchange rates in their header/ticker. ABC Color (abc.com.py) shows USD buy/sell rates for guaranies directly in the page header.

### Key Insight
When planning a business in a specific country, the approach is:
1. Go to the country's top 2-3 news sites directly
2. Search their tag pages for your industry (e.g., /tag/starbucks/, /tag/cafeterias/)
3. Extract verified data: opening dates, investment amounts, locations, employment numbers
4. Check government labor/statistics sites for economic indicators
5. This yields more accurate data than search engines for country-specific business intelligence

## Output Template

When presenting results, include:
- Correct business name (if different from query)
- Owner/professional name and credentials
- Full address
- Phone number(s)
- Rating and review highlights
- Opening hours
- Online presence (Facebook, LinkedIn, website)
- Education/background (if professional practice)
- Any notable details (unclaimed Google listing, etc.)

# Client Business Intelligence

Systematic approach to researching a client business, identifying opportunities, and preparing a pitch.

## Workflow

### Phase 1: Surface-Level Research (30 min)

### Source Priority (run in this order, stop when you have enough)

1. **Their current website** — identify all pages, products, about page, press page, contact. Check for `/nosotros/`, `/tiendas/`, `/prensa/`, `/blog/` — these often have buried gold (subsidiaries, partnerships, director names).
2. **Infonegocios / ABC Color / Última Hora** — PY business press. Use curl + sed for article extraction since these sites are usually accessible without JS. Search: `"{company}" director gerente dueño`
3. **LinkedIn** — `/company/{name}` for corporate page. Note: ALWAYS blocked by auth wall. Can confirm page EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry.
4. **Google Maps** — `https://www.google.com/maps/search/{company}+Paraguay/`. Use the browser tool, then extract `[role=feed]` entries for store names, addresses, ratings, phones.
5. **Facebook** — `/pages/{name}` or `/{name}`. Usually accessible, basic info visible.
6. **DuckDuckGo lite** — `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={query}'` — no-JS. **Warning:** DDG lite now frequently returns CAPTCHA from VPS IPs after 1-2 queries. Don't retry more than twice. If CAPTCHA'd, move on.\n7. **Bing** — fallback when Google blocks. `/search?q={query}` with a desktop UA usually works.\n8. **Wikipedia** — Reliable, always accessible from VPS. Good for company/industry research (FetLife, Lovehoney, Kink.com all have detailed articles). Use browser_navigate.\n9. **Corporate registry** — SET RUC (`dnit.gov.py/web/portal-institucional/consulta-ruc?ruc={RUC}`), Mercantil Registry, InfoCiudad. WARNING: These almost always return 404. Try once then move on.

### Search Queries to Try

```bash
# Ownership/directors
"{company} dueños directores familia"
"{company} gerente director"
"{company} nombre del dueño"

# Subsidiaries
"{company} Argentina filial"
"{company} subsidiaria"

# Press articles
"\"{company}\" Infonegocios"
"\"{company}\" ABC Color"
"\"{company}\" Última Hora"

# General when others fail
curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q={company}+Paraguay'
```

**For web scraping without getting blocked:**
- Use `curl -sL 'https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...' -H 'User-Agent: Mozilla/5.0'` for search results
- Use `curl -sL 'https://lite.duckduckgo.com/lite?q=...'` for simple text extraction
- For blocked sites: Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora are good sources for PY corporate news
- LinkedIn auth wall always blocks — note that the profile EXISTS but can't read without login
- Google blocks aggressive IPs — use Bing or DDG as fallback

### Phase 2: Audit Existing Research Against Reality

**Before re-researching from scratch, check what's already documented and whether it's still accurate.**

1. **Load existing repo docs** — read any `docs/`, `contacts/`, `analysis/` files. Extract claims about: company size, competitor names, pricing, subsidiaries, directors.
2. **Verify live presence** — check DNS/Docker/GitHub for deployment status. `curl -sI domain.com` for HTTP status. Check if their own website still works (competitors may have gone offline).
3. **Cross-reference competitor claims** — a competitor listed as "HIGH threat" may have a dead domain. An "imported brand" may not actually sell in PY anymore. Verify each claim with a live URL check.
4. **Check product images** — if the site has placeholder images reused across products, flag as critical gap.
5. **Document what changed** — the existing docs are a snapshot, not the truth. Note every discrepancy.

### Phase 3: Deep Research (1-2 hours)

1. **Read press articles** — search Infonegocios, ABC Color, Última Hora for interviews with directors
2. **Extract key quotes** — director strategy quotes are gold for understanding priorities
3. **Map corporate structure** — parent, subsidiaries, strategic partners, major clients
4. **Product family analysis** — every product line, cross-sell pairs, pricing tiers, warranty differences
5. **Competitive landscape** — list direct competitors with VERIFIED live websites. DNS-check each one. Note dead domains as opportunities, not gaps.

### Phase 3: Opportunity Analysis

1. **Cross-sell math** — calculate the upsell potential per customer (e.g., mattress + base + protector + almohada = +Gs. 1.17M)
2. **B2B opportunities** — wholesale client portals, hotel/hospitality programs, government contracts
3. **Market gaps** — what competitors don't do that this client could
4. **Geographic expansion** — Argentina subsidiary (if exists), Brazil border cities, Mercosur advantages

### Phase 4: Contact Documentation

1. **Research the contact** — LinkedIn, news mentions, Google
2. **Document what's known vs unknown** — be honest about gaps
3. **Map authority level** — can they make decisions? Who else needs to be involved?
4. **Build relationship strategy** — warm intro? Cold outreach? Existing relationship?

### Phase 5: Pitch Preparation

Create `contacts/deal-strategy.md` with:
- Deal options (tiered from low to high scope)
- Step-by-step conversation flow (open with value → show → ask → present opportunity → close)
- Talking points (what to highlight, what NOT to say)
- Objection handling for likely concerns
- Competitive positioning (why us vs agencies)
- Pricing signals (monthly retainer, project-based, revenue share)

## File Structure for Client Documentation

```
{client-repo}/
├── contacts/
│   ├── README.md              # Index of all contacts & documents
│   ├── {contact-name}.md       # Profile + relationship strategy
│   └── deal-strategy.md        # Pitch guide + deal options
├── docs/
│   ├── business-analysis.md    # Corporate research
│   └── opportunity-analysis.md # Deal opportunities
```

### Service-at-Home Safety Protocol

When the client offers a **mobile/at-home service** (depilación a domicilio, delivery, etc.), proactively build a safety protocol addressing:

**Risk:** "Alguien pide, voy y me roban."

**Mitigation layers:**
1. **Pre-verification (call, not just text)** — Llmar antes de ir. Si no contesta, no vas.
2. **Location sharing** — Compartir ubicación en vivo con contacto de confianza.
3. **Check-in/out protocol** — "Voy a [dirección]" → "Entré" → "Salí bien". Timer de 30 min.
4. **Intuition rule** — Si algo se siente mal al llegar, cancelás. Decís "perdón, me surgió un problema".
5. **First-session filter** — Ofrecer primera sesión gratis/descuento profundo a las primeras X personas. Sirve como filtro y genera confianza mutua.
6. **No carry cash** — Preferir transferencia bancaria. Menos incentivo al robo.

Documentar en `docs/safety-protocol.md` en el repo del cliente. Incluir checklist diario imprimible y plan de emergencia (qué hacer si pasa algo). Ver `references/service-at-home-safety.md` para template completo.

---

### Phase 6: Service Expansion Research (new service line for existing client)

When a client with an existing business wants to add a NEW service line targeting an underserved segment (e.g., affirmative depilation for trans women, men's grooming, post-op care):

**Step 1: 20-Question Structured Questionnaire with Multiple-Choice Options**

Format: Markdown with 4 blocks of 5 questions each (20 total). Each question has 4-5 pre-generated options (a/b/c/d/e). The MC format forces the client to decide fast instead of rambling.

**Important: include blank "Otro: ________" lines** — clients often combine or modify options. Document their exact words, not the closest option.

Blocks are always:
- Block 1 (Q1-4): Validación personal / Motivación
- Block 2 (Q5-8): Equipo / Técnica
- Block 3 (Q9-12): Precios / Modelo de negocio
- Block 4 (Q13-16): Marketing / Contacto comunitario
- Block 5 (Q17-20): Seguridad / Privacidad / Riesgos

After the client responds, **make the remaining decisions as IA** — don't send the parsed answers back for approval. The client said "decide todo como IA". This includes: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy variations. Document the reasoning for each decision.

If the client leaves a question open ("investiga online y decide"), research and decide. Don't ask again.

2. **Análisis de respuestas** — Las respuestas del dueño revelan prioridades, presupuesto, conocimientos, y miedos. Documentar textualmente, incluyendo correcciones y anotaciones que el dueño agrega a las opciones pre-generadas. Extract all "decide todo como IA" signals and act on them.

3. **Research en 7 lotes paralelos** — Dividir los 100+ temas en lotes: A) Mercado/competencia, B) Máquina/equipo, C) Comunidad, D) Médico, E) Marketing, F) Finanzas, G) Psicología, H) Logística. Investigar cada lote y compilar en un documento master.

4. **Competitor pricing via browser scraping** — Para clínicas/competidores PY:
   - Abrir con `browser_navigate` los sitios de competidores directos
   - Usar `browser_snapshot(full=true)` para capturar precios, zonas, packs
   - Extraer precios reales visibles en el HTML (ej: DepilArte tenía Gs. 49,000-220,000 visibles)
   - Si el sitio está bloqueado, probar `curl -sL` con User-Agent desktop o DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`https://html.duckduckgo.com/html?q=...`)
   - Para sitios sin precios visibles, estimar basado en: tamaño de zona (mini/pink/pequeña/mediana/especial/grande), y precio promedio de mercado PY

5. **Search engine evasion** — Google, Bing, Brave, and Ecosia all block VPS IPs after 1-2 requests. DuckDuckGo HTML mode (`html.duckduckgo.com/html`) works but also blocks after ~3 requests with a CAPTCHA. Recovery strategies:
   - Send requests with rotated User-Agent headers via `curl`
   - Use Browserbase (when available) which routes through residential proxies
   - Fall back to training data knowledge for common business intelligence topics (Paraguay market, IPL pricing, trans community orgs)
   - Accept blocked results as "not accessible via automation" rather than retrying endlessly

6. **Master document compilation** — Después de investigar todos los lotes, compilar en `docs/trans-research-master.md` (o nombre análogo) con:
   - Cada lote como sección A-H
   - Una tabla de resumen ejecutivo al final con decisiones clave (precio, descuento, marketing, plan)
   - Subir a repo y dar link al dueño

7. **100-item research checklist** — After the master document, generate a separate file `docs/{topic}-research-100-list.md` with 100 specific questions/items organized in the same categories. This serves as: progress tracker, idea source for next iterations, and documentation of research scope. Each item is 1 sentence.

8. **Website audit for existing clients** — When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:
   - **Structure map** — every page, component, and content file
   - **Content audit** — es.json (or equivalent): find placeholders ("****"), duplicates (two FAQ sections), missing sections (no mention of new service)
   - **UX/Design** — photos vs emoji, social proof, about section, favicon
   - **SEO** — sitemap, robots, og:image, meta tags, schema
   - **Performance** — DOM size, load time, cache headers
   - **Score each category** 1-10 with justification
   - **Prioritized improvement plan** — 3 tiers: 🔴 esta semana, 🟡 este mes, 🟢 cuando haya tiempo. Each item has: effort estimate, impact assessment, specific files to change.

9. **Action plan from audit** — After the audit:
   - Decide all pricing/package decisions as IA
   - Generate a 7-day checklist (día por día) 
   - Generate a 30-day plan with milestones
   - Generate WhatsApp messages ready to send (4 templates: new client, confirmation, 24h follow-up, 20-day reminder)
   - Push all docs to repo, give client the GitHub link

## Pitfalls

- **LinkedIn always requires login** — accept that you'll only see the auth wall. Note the profile EXISTS but can't read content. Don't retry multiple times.
- **Instagram handle ≠ correct name** — e.g., an artist's studio has IG @ocho.estudio but the correct name is "Estudio 8", not "Ocho Estudio". Always verify the actual name from the artist's own materials (interviews quotes, personal bio), not from the Instagram handle or press articles that copy the handle.
- **Press articles may use wrong name** — journalists often copy the Instagram handle without verifying. An article might say "Ocho Estudio" when the real name is "Estudio 8". Preserve the press's wording in article excerpts (they wrote that, not us) but use the correct name in your own site content (bio, timeline, nav).
- **City list vs country count** — press articles often give explicit city lists that contradict the site's broad claim (e.g., site says "10 countries" but press mentions 21+ specific cities). When cross-referencing, prefer the article's granular data. The artist's actual reach is usually understated in a terse site stat.
- **Cross-reference built site against research** — when an existing site is already built, don't just research in isolation. Produce a gap analysis: "what's in the built site vs what research reveals." This includes: works present but lacking images, works missing entirely, scale understated, press underutilized. The deliverable is a structured comparison, not a blank-slate recommendation.
- **Instagram blocks all automated access** — `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE` always. Don't waste time retrying Instagram directly. See `references/instagram-osint.md` for workarounds.
- **Google blocks aggressively** — after 2-3 searches from the same IP, you get a CAPTCHA wall. Switch to DDG lite or Bing immediately.
- **PY corporate registries are unreliable** — SET (DNIT), Mercantil, InfoCiudad often return 404. News articles are more reliable than official registries. Always try once but don't spend more than 30 seconds.
- **Paraguay business owners often have low digital footprint** — many executives have no LinkedIn, no social media. "No public results found" is a valid finding, not a failed search.
- **Warm leads are better than cold research** — if the user has a direct contact (like Leticia Roig as GM of Superspuma), prioritize documenting what they know over searching publicly.
- **Don't rely on browser for everything** — Curl with simple User-Agent headers often works where the headless browser gets blocked. Try `curl -sL` before opening the browser for each source.
- **Infonegocios articles are gold** — they often have director interviews with strategy quotes, company ambitions, competitive claims. Extract the full article text, not just snippets.
- **See `references/search-engine-evasion-pattern.md`** for the full ordered fallback chain when Google/DDG/Bing/Brave all block automated queries. Yahoo is the most reliable VPS-search-engine as of 2026-05. Wikipedia API always works. Instagram data can be extracted from Yahoo search result snippets.

### Pitfall: Template/boilerplate leftovers

When the client site was generated from a multi-tenant builder like `paragu-ai-builder`, check for **stale template files** in `lib/client-kit/` (or equivalent). These are the generic versions of the actual components in `components/`. They are NOT used by the live site but confuse future edits. Recommend cleaning them up in a follow-up commit.

**Detection:** `find . -not -path './node_modules/*' -not -path './.git/*' -not -path './.next/*' -type f | sort` shows component files in TWO locations (e.g. `components/header.tsx` + `lib/client-kit/header.tsx`). The `components/` versions are the live ones.

## Website Audit (for existing clients)

When the client already has a live site, run a full audit covering:

### Structure map
- Every page, component, and content file. Output: tree of `app/`, `components/`, `content/`.

### Content audit
- Check `es.json` (or equivalent): find **placeholders** (`"****"`, `"placeholder": true`), **duplicates** (two FAQ arrays), **missing sections** (no mention of new service line), **stale references**.
- Check every page — does it load? Does it have content?

### UX/Design scoring
- Score each category 1-10: Funcionalidad, Diseño, Contenido, Prueba Social, SEO, Performance, UX Móvil
- Justify each score in 1 sentence
- Identify the biggest gap (typically "Contenido" or "Prueba Social")

### SEO checklist
- Favicon? (link rel="icon" in head)
- og:image for social shares?
- Sitemap.xml? robots.txt?
- Canonical URL?
- JSON-LD schema?

### Performance
- Load time (from browser performance API)
- DOM size
- Cache headers
- HTTP protocol (h3 is good)

### Prioritized improvement plan
3 tiers with effort estimate and impact:
- 🔴 **Critical** (hacer esta semana) — e.g., favicon, placeholder fix, new service section, Instagram link, photo of owner
- 🟡 **Medium** (hacer este mes) — e.g., real photos, og:image, unified FAQs, social proof counters
- 🟢 **Low** (cuando haya tiempo) — e.g., blog, dark mode, animations, chatbot

Each item should specify the file(s) to change and the exact line/region.

### Implementation after audit

When the client says "implementa todos" or "decide todo como IA":
1. Apply ALL changes identified in the audit
2. For pricing/service decisions left open by the client: research, decide, document reasoning
3. Build: `npm run build` (or equivalent) and fix any errors
4. Git commit + push
5. Docker build + deploy
6. If deploy fails (resources), note it and tell the client what manual step is needed

### Visual Artist Research

When researching a visual artist (muralist, painter, illustrator) for a website project, use `references/artist-visual-research-pattern.md` for the full ordered workflow. Key differences from business research:

- **No corporate registry** — no RUC, no SET. Use Wikidata + Wikipedia API + news sites.
- **Press articles are the richest source** — interviews reveal style, influences, quotes, career timeline.
- **YouTube is underutilized** — extraction of `ytInitialData` from HTML gives video interviews that news articles don't cover.
- **Name confusion is common** — verify with 2+ sources before concluding. Paraguayan artists may have 1-2 press articles but no Wikipedia presence.
- **Website plan is the deliverable** — unlike business research (deal strategy), artist research produces a complete website architecture (10 pages minimum) with content specification per page, tech stack, monetization strategy, and roadmap.

## Per-session reference file management

After each major research session for a client:
1. Create `references/{client}-{topic}-{date}.md` with condensed findings
2. This file is the **session transcript summary** — not a substitute for the full docs in the client repo
3. Keep it focused on: key findings, techniques learned, decisions that future sessions should know
4. Delete the oldest reference when the list gets too long (5+ files) — the repo docs are the source of truth

## Full-Org Infrastructure Inventory

Use `references/batch-infrastructure-inventory.md` when you need to map ALL repos, Docker services, cron jobs, and sites at once (e.g., creating a /root/priorities/ repo). This is NOT a client-specific skill — it's the meta-inventory that discovers every asset the user owns. Run the batch commands there, classify every repo, extract all Docker URLs, then produce the multi-file structured deliverable.

### Key pattern: per-repo framework detection from package.json deps

When batch-scanning 30+ repos, detect framework from dependency versions:

```python
deps = {**pkg.get("dependencies", {}), **pkg.get("devDependencies", {})}
if "next" in deps:
    v = deps["next"].lstrip("^~")
    framework = f"Next.js {v.split('.')[0]}" if v else "Next.js"
elif "nuxt" in deps: framework = "Nuxt"
elif "react" in deps: framework = "React"
```

This gives you per-repo tech stack without opening every single config file.

## `decide-todo-como-IA` pattern

When the client responds to the 20-question questionnaire and leaves decisions open with "decide todo como IA" or "investiga online y decide":
- Research each open question (competitor pricing, technical specs, community contacts)
- Take a firm decision — don't propose options back
- Document the reasoning for each decision in markdown
- Include a table: "Decisiones de la IA basadas en investigación"
- This applies to: final pricing, package structures, what's included in each service, which zones get discounts, marketing copy

## Verification

After creating the documentation files:
1. Push to repo: `git add contacts/ docs/ && git commit && git push`
2. Verify the site content reflects the research (update es.json with new company info)
3. If adding stores with GPS, add Google Maps links to the store locator page
4. Cross-sell opportunities should be reflected in product page design (show bundles)

{"success": true, "name": "client-business-intelligence", "file": "references/instagram-osint.md", "content": "# Instagram OSINT Workflow

Research an Instagram-only brand/organization when direct Instagram access is blocked.

## The Problem

Instagram blocks all automated access:
- Browser automation: `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE`
- Direct curl: empty response or login wall
- Third-party viewers (imginn, dumpoir, instasave, etc.): Cloudflare + rate limited

## The Workaround: Search Engine Snippet Extraction

Search engines cache Instagram profile data and post excerpts. **Yahoo Search** reliably renders these snippets even when Google, Bing, and DuckDuckGo are blocked.

### Step 1: Identify the exact handle

If the user provides an Instagram URL, extract the handle:
- `https://www.instagram.com/30vcs_cannabis_club/` → `30vcs_cannabis_club`

If the user says "30VGS" and the IG handle is different ("30vcs"), note the difference — the user may have mis-typed it.

### Step 2: Search on Yahoo

Open a browser to:
```
https://search.yahoo.com/search?p={handle}&n=20
```

Use `n=20` to get more results per page.

### Step 3: Extract profile data

Yahoo results pages for Instagram handles show a snippet with:

**Profile data** (from the profile page result):
- Follower count
- Following count
- Post count
- Bio text
- Link in bio

**Post excerpts** (from individual post results):
- Like count
- Comment count
- Post text content (up to ~200 chars)
- Date posted
- Hashtags and @mentions

Example from this session:
```
211 likes, 14 comments - 30vcs_cannabis_club on December 23, 2025: "Con este line up sabes que vamos a pasar brutal🔥 Acordate 🔥 30vcs x Sublime 🔥 Feria + fiesta + música en vivo 📍 Piko Studio 📆 26 de diciembre ⏰ Desde las 16:20 (420 pm)".
```

### Step 4: Identify related accounts

Search engine results reveal:
- **@mentions in post text** → collaborators, venues, sponsors
- **Related searches** → Yahoo generates "searches related to" showing possible associated terms (check these — they're algorithmically generated but can surface patterns)
- **Cross-posts** → Facebook posts that mention the IG handle

### Step 5: Cross-reference with other sources

| Source | What to look for | How |
|--------|-----------------|-----|
| Facebook | Event pages, cross-posts mentioning IG handle | Yahoo search: `{handle} facebook` |
| News articles | Media coverage of events | Yahoo search: `{handle} {city} feria evento` |
| Google Maps | Physical venue if they host events | `maps.google.com/maps/search/{venue}+{city}` |
| TikTok | Related event content | Included in Yahoo universal results |

### Step 6: Media articles about the organization

Search news sites directly:
```
https://search.yahoo.com/search?p={org name}+feria+evento+paraguay&n=20
```

Follow the links to media coverage (use browser_navigate). Some sites may return 404 if the article was moved — search engine snippets still retain useful description text.

### Step 7: Compile the profile

Combine all sources into a structured profile:

| Field | Source |
|-------|--------|
| Handle | Instagram URL |
| Followers | Yahoo snippet |
| Bio | Yahoo snippet |
| Events | Instagram posts + news articles |
| Partners | @mentions in posts + news |
| Venue | Instagram location tags |
| Merch | Bio link + secondary accounts |

## Pitfalls

- **Yahoo results may take 2-3 page loads** — sometimes the first load shows only the header. Scroll down or re-navigate.
- **Related searches are algorithmic, not evidence** — Yahoo generates them based on keyword proximity, not actual content. Don't cite them as sources.
- **Instagram handles vs tech terms** — "30VGS" returns electronics (MOSFET voltage). "30VCS" returns the cannabis club. Always verify the exact handle.
- **Articles disappear** — Mariguay.com articles about the same events returned 404 on direct access but could still be read through Yahoo's cached snippet. Save the snippet text.
- **No way to get follower growth or engagement rate** — Yahoo only shows current snapshot, not historical data.
- **Post content is truncated** — Yahoo shows ~150-200 chars per post. Full text requires visiting the post (blocked).

## When to Use This vs Other Approaches

| Situation | Method |
|-----------|--------|
| Brand has a public IG handle | This Yahoo snippet workflow |
| Brand has a Facebook page | Facebook pages are usually accessible via browser |
| Brand is on LinkedIn | Only confirm existence (login wall). Skip content extraction. |
| Brand has a website | Direct browser navigation + curl extraction |
| Brand has news coverage | Search by company name on Yahoo + follow links |

How to research a visual artist (muralist, painter, illustrator) in-depth when search engines block automated queries.

## Sources ordered by resilience

| Source | Block risk | What you get |
|--------|-----------|--------------|
| Wikipedia API | None | Bio, style, notable works, exhibitions |
| Wikidata API | None | Structured data, links to all external sources |
| YouTube API (search) | Low | Video titles, channel names, interviews |
| News sites directly (ABC, ÚH, Infonegocios etc.) | Low | Press articles, interviews, exhibition coverage |
| Graffitimundo.com | None | Curated artist interviews (LATAM specialists) |
| ArtStation / Behance | Medium | Portfolio pages |
| Instagram (browser) | Always fails | Use search engine snippets instead |
| Google / Bing / DDG (automated) | Always blocks VPS | Use curl with rotated UAs, or give up fast |

## Ordered research workflow (30-40 min)

1. **Wikipedia API** — `https://en.wikipedia.org/w/api.php?action=query&list=search&srsearch={artist}&format=json&origin=*` — 0% blocked
2. **Google Knowledge Graph** — check if the artist has a kgmid (from initial search payload). This gives structured summary, social links, quick facts.
3. **Wikidata** — `https://www.wikidata.org/w/api.php?action=wbsearchentities&search={artist}&language=en&format=json&origin=*` — links to all external profiles
4. **Direct news sites** — ABC Color, Última Hora, Infonegocios, Pausa, La Nación — use `browser_navigate` directly. These PY news sites are usually accessible.
5. **Graffitimundo** — excellent curated interviews with Latin American street artists
6. **YouTube search** — `https://www.youtube.com/results?search_query={artist}+paraguay` — extract `ytInitialData` from HTML with regex. This works 100% without auth.
7. **Social media from search snippets** — Instagram and Facebook are blocked. Get info from Google/Bing cached snippets.
8. **Google Maps** — for mural locations, use `browser_navigate` to search maps

## What to extract for a complete artist profile

### Identity
- Full name, birth year, birthplace
- Artistic name/nickname, social handles
- Education (formal or autodidact)
- Current residence

### Career timeline
- First artistic experiences (age, medium)
- Key milestones year by year
- First exhibition / first mural
- When and how they evolved style
- Study/studio founded

### Artistic style
- Self-described labels (or labels they reject)
- Visual influences (comics, classical art, pop culture, indigenous)
- Technical signature (can control, color palette, linework, composition)
- Themes and motifs (identity, environment, social justice, mythology)
- Evolution of style over time

### Notable works
- Each work: title, year, location, dimensions, description
- Commission context (commercial vs personal vs exhibition)
- Materials and technique
- Community/social impact

### Exhibitions
- Solo shows (name, year, gallery, description)
- Group shows
- International presentations

### Press coverage
- Major articles with links
- Cover stories (these are highest value)
- International coverage

### International presence
- Countries where they've exhibited/painted
- Collaborations with international artists
- Residencies

### Entrepreneurial projects
- Studios founded
- Shops/stores
- Collaborations with brands

### Social media
- Instagram: username, follower count, posting frequency
- Facebook: page or personal
- YouTube: channel or guest appearances
- Website: does it exist? (this is the client need)

## Building the website plan

After collecting the above, synthesize into:

1. **Current state diagnosis** — what they have, what's missing
2. **10-page architecture** with content per page
3. **Tech stack recommendation** (Next.js + Tailwind for artists)
4. **Monetization** (prints, merchandising, mural commissions, workshops, walking tours)
5. **SEO / content strategy** (blog content categories, bilingual if applicable)
6. **Feature priorities** (map of mural locations is unique value)
7. **Roadmap** (phased delivery)

## Pitfalls

- Don't confuse similar names (Oz Montanía ≠ Ozz Montoya ≠ Oz Montoya). Always verify with at least 2 sources.
- Paraguayan artists may have more press coverage than Wikipedia presence. Check ABC, ÚH, Pausa before concluding "no information found."
- Instagram always blocks automated browser access. Don't retry more than once. Get info from news article mentions of their Instagram.
- Some artists work under multiple handles (e.g., Instagram @ozmontania, Facebook /pyrotaurus). Check both.
- For muralists specifically: look for "muralista paraguayo" not just the artist name in searches — sometimes the name is only mentioned in articles that also use the category label.
- Google Captcha blocks VPS IPs indefinitely with the `/sorry/` redirect. Once blocked, no retry works. Switch sources immediately.
- **IG handle ≠ correct name** — an artist's studio might have IG @ocho.estudio but the correct name is "Estudio 8". Verify from the artist's own words in interviews, not from handles or press articles that copy handles.
- **Press may use wrong naming** — journalists often copy Instagram handles without verifying. "Ocho Estudio" in press doesn't mean the studio is actually named that. Preserve press wording in article excerpts but use the correct name in the site's own content.
- **City list vs country count** — press gives specific cities (21+) that contradict a site's vague stat ("10 countries"). Prefer the granular data. The artist's real reach is almost always understated.
- **Cross-reference build vs research** — when an existing site is already built, produce a gap analysis comparing what's in the codebase vs what research reveals. Don't treat the site as authoritative; it may have 6 works when the artist has 20+. The gap analysis IS the deliverable.
- **Behance returns polluted results** — searching "Oz Montania" on Behance gives Wizard of Oz fan art, not the artist. Don't waste time there for Paraguayan/LATAM artists.

Instagram blocks all automated access:
- Browser automation: `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE`
- Direct curl: empty response or login wall
- Third-party viewers (imginn, dumpoir, instasave, etc.): Cloudflare + rate limited", "new_string": "## The Problem

Instagram blocks all automated access:
- Browser automation: `net::ERR_HTTP_RESPONSE_CODE_FAILURE`
- Direct curl: empty response or login wall
- Third-party viewers (imginn, dumpoir, instasave, etc.): Cloudflare + rate limited

## First Resort: Instagram's Internal API

Before resorting to search engine snippets, try Instagram's own API endpoint. It's undocumented but returns full profile JSON when called with a mobile User-Agent header — no auth required for public profiles.

```
curl -s --max-time 10 \
  -H 'User-Agent: Instagram 219.0.0.12.117 Android' \
  'https://i.instagram.com/api/v1/users/web_profile_info/?username={HANDLE}'
```

Returns: id, full_name, biography (full text), edge_followed_by (follower count), edge_follow (following count), is_private, is_verified, is_professional_account, category_name, profile_pic_url (high-res), external_url, bio_links array (with titles and URLs), biography_with_entities (mentions and hashtags parsed), has_clips, highlight_reel_count.

### Limitations of this API:
- Only returns profile metadata — **no posts/media/gallery data** (that requires the `/feed` or `/media` endpoints which need auth)
- You get follower/following counts but NOT individual followers or recent posts
- The profile picture is a low-res thumbnail URL — you'd need to construct the high-res version from the URL pattern
- Works for public profiles only (private profiles return a different response with `is_private: true` and minimal data)
- The API endpoint may change; `web_profile_info` has been stable as of 2026

### When to use this:
- When you need the full bio text (search snippets truncate)
- When you need bio links/Linktree URLs
- When you need the exact follower count
- When you need to verify if the account is professional/business/verified
- As FIRST STEP — it's fast (< 1s), requires no browser, and gives richer data than any snippet approach"}

{"action": "patch", "file_path": "references/instagram-osint.md", "name": "client-business-intelligence", "new_string": "## Step 5: Cross-reference with Linktree / bio links

The Instagram API returns `bio_links` array with titles and URLs. If the user has a Linktree (very common for artists/creators), the Linktree page often exposes **JSON-LD** structured data that reveals their most important links without needing to render JavaScript.

From a Linktree page HTML, extract with Python:
```python
import re, json
matches = re.findall(r'<script type="application/ld\+json"[^>]*>(.*?)</script>', html, re.DOTALL)
for m in matches:
    data = json.loads(m)
    # 'significantLink' field often contains the primary external link (book, store, portfolio)
    print(data.get('significantLink'))
    # 'mainEntity.sameAs' array may have social links even if empty
    print(data.get('mainEntity', {}).get('sameAs', []))
```

This is useful because Linktree pages are JS-rendered (blocking full content extraction), but the JSON-LD in the static HTML loads immediately and often contains the most important link.

Also check the Archive.org metadata for:
- Full author name (often in the uploader's profile or the item description)
- Edition information
- Companion content items (sometimes the book has a separate companion upload)", "old_string": "## Step 5: Cross-reference with other sources"}
